coredata.dc:title: High temperature hydrothermal etching of g-C3N4 for synthesis of N doped carbon quantum dots-supported CdS photocatalyst to enhance visible light driven hydrogen generation
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhenning
Wang, Yanhong
Zhuang, Jiayuan
Li, Yawen
Jia, Lishan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we proposed a facile method to synthesize N doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using g-C3N4 as carbon-nitrogen source, and the NCQDs-modified CdS nanocomposite catalysts for photocatalytic water splitting were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The results showed that the NCQDs with a size of 3.2 nm obtained by hydrothermal etching were adsorbed on the surface of CdS particles with a size of 80 nm and induced the regulation of the surface morphology of CdS particles. There was strong interaction between NCQDs and CdS through C-S bond. The introduction of NCQDs in composite catalysts not only increased the photo-absorption performance but also suppressed the recombination of photo-generated carriers, which enabled the photo-generated electrons to transfer to NCQDs effectively and quickly and improved the photocatalytic performance of hydrogen production from water splitting. The composite photocatalysts showed good response to visible light in UV-Vis DRS and the photocatalytic water splitting experiment showed that the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of composite catalysts was significantly improved under visible light (λ > 400 nm). And the maximum hydrogen production rate reached 2306.1 μmol/g/h, which was about 7 times the amount of unsupported NCQDs sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCQDS
CdS
Photosensitizer
Photocatalytic hydrogen production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the structures and morphologies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the general program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21878251 , 22078271 ). The authors thank Analysis and Testing Center of Xiamen University for the analysis and observation work in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a,b) and TEM (c,d) images of g-C3N4; TEM images of C-200 (e), C-210 (f), C-230 (g) and C-250 (h) and corresponding particle size profile (i–l), XRD of g-C3N4 (m).
The C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s high resolution XPS sprctra of C-200 (a), C-210 (b), C-230 (c) and C-250 (d).
XRD patterns of d-CdS, CdS-xC and g-C3N4 photocatalysts.
SEM images of d-CdS (a) and CdS-50C (b); TEM images of d-CdS (c) and CdS-50C (d); HRTEM images (e,f) and EDS pattern of CdS-50C (g).
N2 adsorption-desorption curves (a) and pore size distribution of d-CdS and CdS-50C.
FTIR spectra (a) and Raman spectra (b) of synthesized photocatalysts.

XPS survey spectra of d-CdS and CdS-50C (a); high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d for d-CdS and CdS-50C (b); high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p for d-CdS and CdS-50C (c); high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s for CdS-50C (d).

Photocatalytic H2 production activity of synthesized photocatalysts (a); cycling photocatalytic H2 production tests for CdS-50C and d-CdS (b); XRD patterns for CdS-50C before and after photocatalysis (c).

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a) and Kubelk-Munk curves of synthesized photocatalysts (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
PL spectra of synthesized photocatalysts (a); time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra of d-CdS and CdS-50C (b).
The transient photocurrent response of synthesized photocatalysts (a); EIS Nyquist plots of synthesized photocatalysts (b); cycling voltammetry curves (c) of d-CdS and CdS-50C.
M-S curves (a) and VB-XPS spectra of d-CdS and CdS-50C.
Photocatalytic H2 production mechanism of the composite photocatalyst.
The calculated band gap values of photocatalysts according to Kubelk-Munk curves.
Corresponding parameters determined from double exponential function and calculated average radiative lifetime.

head.ce:title: High temperature hydrothermal etching of g-C3N4 for synthesis of N doped carbon quantum dots-supported CdS photocatalyst to enhance visible light driven hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen, Fujian 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen, Fujian 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we proposed a facile method to synthesize N doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using g-C3N4 as carbon-nitrogen source, and the NCQDs-modified CdS nanocomposite catalysts for photocatalytic water splitting were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The results showed that the NCQDs with a size of 3.2 nm obtained by hydrothermal etching were adsorbed on the surface of CdS particles with a size of 80 nm and induced the regulation of the surface morphology of CdS particles. There was strong interaction between NCQDs and CdS through C-S bond. The introduction of NCQDs in composite catalysts not only increased the photo-absorption performance but also suppressed the recombination of photo-generated carriers, which enabled the photo-generated electrons to transfer to NCQDs effectively and quickly and improved the photocatalytic performance of hydrogen production from water splitting. The composite photocatalysts showed good response to visible light in UV-Vis DRS and the photocatalytic water splitting experiment showed that the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of composite catalysts was significantly improved under visible light (λ > 400 nm). And the maximum hydrogen production rate reached 2306.1 μmol/g/h, which was about 7 times the amount of unsupported NCQDs sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production derived from solar energy is considered to be one of the most promising technologies for clean hydrogen energy production. Recently, the visible light responsive CdS is regarded as a potential photocatalytic material for it is narrow energy band gap (2.4 eV) and suitable conduction band position (−0.51 V vs. SHE), which can effectively respond to visible light and meet the thermodynamic requirements of photocatalytic water splitting [1]. It has attracted extensive attention from researchers, and is applied in diversified fields including photocatalytic water splitting, photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, photoelectricity sensor etc. [2–6]. Unfortunately, photocatalytic activity of CdS is unsatisfactory owing to the fast recombination of charge carriers and photo-corrosion. At present, the focus of attention for photocatalyst is how to improve the absorption of visible light and to effectively inhibit the recombination of photo-generated carriers. Usually, noble metals or their alloys are used as cocatalysts to enhance the separation of photo-generated charge carriers [7–10]. Merely, the scarcity and high cost of noble metals hinder their commercialization.

In view of the limitation of noble metals, it is a feasible strategy to develop non-metallic materials as cocatalysts. Referring to non-metallic material, carbon -based materials can be exploited to serve as cocatalysts for photocatalyst due to their excellent electrical conductivity and abundant sources. For example, it has been reported that graphene oxide (GO), carbon fiber, carbon ball and CQDs are used as cocatalysts to enhance photocatalytic activity [11–16]. Among those carbon materials, CQDs are regarded as a promising material as a result of excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and photostability property [17]. CQDs are sp3 hybridized and amorphous and usually less than 10 nm [18–20], and have a great potential application prospect in photocatalysis for it is excellent electron transfer and reservoir properties, which is conductive to enhance photocatalytic activity. For instance, Sui et al. reported that CQDs/TiO2 nanosheets presented higher photocatalytic H2 evolution than pure TiO2 for the CQDs play a key role of electron reservoir [12]. Besides, Qiu et al. reported that CQDs enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CdS [21–24].

Among the synthesis method of CQDs, one of the popular methods is hydrothermal for it is convenience and efficiency. And organic compounds are used as precursors, which generally contain benzenoid structure, such as 1,2,4-triaminobenzene and o-xylene were reported to be used as precursors [25,26]. It is well known that g-C3N4 is a kind of abundant carbon-nitrogen polymer functional material with 3s-triazine ring structure unit and layered carbon-nitrogen hybrid structure, which is easy to peel off and better controlled synthesis of carbon-based materials [27]. Such as, it has been reported that g-C3N4 could act as carbon source to synthesize g-C3N4-LaCO3OH photocatalysts for photocatalytic NO removal as g-C3N4 decomposed partially and reacted with La precursor to form g-C3N4-LaCO3OH heterojunction [28] and Hao et al. reported using g-C3N4 as precursor to prepare Sm2O(CO3)2 as laser absorbents [29]. It is interesting to note that Zhang et al. reported that some regions on g-C3N4 was decomposed to nitrogen or cyano fragments and residual carbon atoms formed CQDs on g-C3N4 via ultrasonic cavitation effect [30]. These previous reports inspired us that g-C3N4 is unstable under certain conditions, which may be used as carbon-nitrogen source to synthesize NCQDs. Generally, N doping can enhance the electron transport of carbon materials, which is conducive to it is application in photoelectric catalysis [31]. Therefore, g-C3N4 derived NCQDs is expected to be an efficient cocatalyst for photocatalysis.

Herein, we designed and constructed a novel NCQD by direct stripping of g-C3N4 via 230 °C high temperature hydrothermal treatment and applied in CdS photocatalyst as photosensitizer and cocatalyst, which could effectively increase the light absorption and inhibit the recombination of photo-generated carriers of CdS. The CdS modified by NCQDs exhibited outstanding photocatalytic performance for hydrogen production from water splitting. This novel method will contribute to the development of NCQDs/CdS matrix composites.

Thiourea (NH2CSNH2), urea (CH4N2O), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), lactic acid (C3H6O3), Naphthol and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cadmium acetate (Cd(Ac)2`2H2O) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. 1000 Da dialysis bag (Amicon Ultra-4, Millipore). All materials were used without further purification.

Urea was used as raw material to synthesize g-C3N4 nanosheets. 20 g grinded urea was put into a quartz crucible with a lid and heated up to 550 °C for 4 h followed by 500 °C for 2 h in muffle furnace. The calcined light yellow bulk sample was ground to powder and collected to obtain g-C3N4.

NCQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using g-C3N4 as raw material. 1 g-C3N4 powders were added to 20 mL deionized water in a 100 mL Teflon liner with 20 min continuous stirring. Then the kettle was put in stainless steel autoclave and heated at 230 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the resulting solution was filtered three times with 50 nm filter membrane and then was dialyzed (1000 Da) for 12 h. In order to explore the synthesized process of NCQDs from g-C3N4, g-C3N4 was treated at different hydrothermal temperatures (200, 210 and 250 °C, respectively). The samples gained at different hydrothermal temperatures were labeled as C-210, C-230 and C-250, respectively.

CdS was synthesized using traditional solvothermal method. 3 mmol Cd(Ac)2•2H2O and 9 mmol NH2CSNH2 were dispersed in 50 mL ethylenediamine and stirred for 20 min. Then the obtained suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 100 °C for 12 h. The as-prepared yellow sediment was filtered, washed with ethanol and deionized water for server times and dried at 60 °C in oven for 12 h.

NCQDs/CdS composite photocatalysts were prepared using a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.1 g as-prepared CdS powders were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water and sonicated for 30 min, then a series of different amounts g-C3N4 were added into the above suspension followed by another 30 min ultrasonic treatment. Then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 230 °C in oven for 12 h. The as-prepared powder was filtered, washed and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The obtained NCQDs/CdS composite photocatalysts were labeled as CdS-xC (x represents the additive amount of g-C3N4, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 mg, respectively). The control group was synthesized without addition of g-C3N4 via identical methods and labeled as d-CdS.

The chemical composition and phase of the samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV) using Cu Kα as source of X-ray. The microstructure and morphology of the synthesized samples were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai 30). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained on a Nicolet Avatar 330 spectrometer. Horiba Xplora spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm was used for Raman spectroscopic measurement. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured on a nitrogen adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA) at 77 K. Quantum 2000-XPS instrument was applied for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectrum (DRS) was gained on Cary 5000 Varian spectrophotometer. Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer was used to record the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectrum. Time resolved photoluminescence emission (TRPL) spectrum was recorded by FLS980 spectrophotometer with 370 nm as excitation wavelength. The transient photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cycling voltammetry curve (CV) and Mott–Schottky (M-S) plot of photocatalyst were recorded on CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Co., Shanghai, China) with a Pt wire as counter electrode and a Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode as reference electrode in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution, and a 300 W Xe lamp was applied as light source in photocurrent response test. The EIS were performed in the frequency from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. Preparation of working electrode as following: 5 mg of as-synthesized photocatalyst was dispersed in mixed solution containing 300 μL ethanol and 15 μL Nafion and kept under ultrasonic treatment for 5 min, then the obtained slurry was uniformly dropped onto clean ITO glass carefully and dried at 60 °C.

The photocatalytic H2 production of synthesized photocatalyst was carried out under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). For all experiments, 20 mg of photocatalyst was added into 100 mL aqueous solution containing 10 mL lactic acid in a 180 mL quartz reactor and then the quartz reactor was connected to a photocatalytic evaluation instrument (Beijing Education Au-Light Co. Ltd., China). Before illumination, the system was vacuumed with oil pump to remove the air completely. Then the reactor was irradiated from top by a 300 W Xe lamp (equipped with a UV cutoff filter of λ > 400 nm) with constant stirring, and the reaction temperature was kept at 4 °C. The H2 production was monitored and recorded on a gas chromatography (Jingjie-GC9560) equipped with TCD detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the structures and morphologies
ce:section.ce:para: For the prepared g-C3N4 sample, the XRD pattern in Fig. 1 m showed two characteristic diffraction peaks appeared at 13.0 and 27.4°, which were ascribed to (100) and (002) lattice plane of g-C3N4, respectively. The SEM and TEM images of g-C3N4 in Fig. 1a–d presented a curved sheet microstructure with holes. The size of the sheet was about 500 nm, and the thickness was about 20 nm. The TEM images of samples after hydrothermal treatment were shown in Fig. 1e–h. The TEM images showed the morphology of the C-200 presents as large particles with a size of about 26.0 nm (Fig. 1i). Compared with the C-200, the size of C-210, C-230 and C-250 were smaller than that of the C-200, with a size of 5.4, 3.2 and 4.0 nm (Fig. 1j–l), respectively, and they all presented as spherical particles. The above TEM results indicated that samples with different particle sizes could be obtained via hydrothermal treatment of g-C3N4 at different temperatures.

The functional group composition and structure of samples were analyzed through FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2 , the peak at 3400 cm−1 in FTIR spectra was attributed to the absorption vibration of -OH in water, which was related to the residual water in the samples. The peak at 807 cm−1 of g-C3N4 corresponded to the heptazine ring unit in the structure, while the multiple peaks in the region of 1207∼1646 cm−1 originated from the typical stretching vibration of C-N heterocycles in g-C3N4. The FTIR spectrum of g-C3N4 was in good agreement with reported literature [32,33]. For C-200 sample, two absorption peaks in 1207∼1361 cm−1 region disappeared and a new absorption peak appeared at 1113 cm−1 compared with g-C3N4. More obvious change could be seen in C-210 sample. The absorption peak of heptazine ring unit at 807 cm−1 disappeared completely and the absorption vibration of the heterocyclic ring in the region of 1207∼1646 cm−1 also changed significantly with decrease of absorption peak numbers, which indicated that the orderly stacked heptazine ring unit in g-C3N4 changed obviously. Furthermore, the stretching vibrations of heptazine ring units and C-N heterocycles disappeared in C-230 and C-250. The peaks at 1109, 1393, 1630, 2814 and 3146 cm−1 in C-230 and C-250 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C-O, C-N, C=C/C=N, C-H and N-H, respectively. It was worth noting that the relevant literature has reported the preparation of g-C3N4 quantum dots (g-CNQDs) from g-C3N4. g-CNQDs are characterized by having a conjugated heptazine structure with sp2 hybridized C-N heterocycles in the structure [34–36]. The above FTIR spectra showed that C-230 and C-250 do not have corresponding characteristic absorption peaks of g-CNQDs, so the obtained quantum dots were not g-CNQDs but NCQDs. In other words, the NCQDs could be obtained via hydrothermal treatment of g-C3N4 at specific temperature conditions.

XPS was used to determine the composition and element state. As shown in Fig. 3 , the high resolution spectra of C 1 s of all samples had three peaks at 284.8, 285.8 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-O/C-N and O-C=O, respectively. The C 1 s spectra showed that the peak intensity of the obtained samples at 285.8 eV increased as the hydrothermal temperature increased, manifesting that the degree of polymerization and carbonization of NCQDs were increased. Three peaks could be observed clearly near 399.8, 400.4 and 401.4 eV in the N 1 s spectra for all samples, which were matched with pyrrole N, graphite N and N-H, demonstrating N element was doped into NCQDs. Notably, the peak at 398.3 eV for the original pyridine N disappeared compared with the N 1 s of g-C3N4, which implied that the bond of pyridine N was easily broken and the C-N heterocycle structure of g-C3N4 was easily destroyed during the hydrothermal process leading to the gradual destruction of the heptazine ring and the formation of NCQDs. In addition, the N 1 s spectra also showed that the peak intensity of various components N changed at various temperatures. With the increase of temperature, the peak area of N-H in the N 1 s spectra increased, indicating that the content of N-H increased. The O 1 s spectra for C-200, C-210 and C-230 were deconvoluted into C=O (532.0 eV) and C-O/C-O-C (533.4 eV), respectively, while the C=O and C-O/C-O-C for C-250 were located at 531.0 and 532.6 eV. The negative shift of O 1 s binding energy for C-250 might be due to the partial reduction of NCQDs at high temperature. According to the FTIR and XPS results above, g-C3N4 was gradually decomposed and further polymerized to form NCQDs with the increase of hydrothermal temperature, and there was hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino functional groups on the surface of NCQDs.

The crystalline phases of synthesized samples were investigated by XRD. The diffraction patterns in Fig. 4 showed that d-CdS had 7 peaks located at 24.9°, 26.6°, 28.2°, 36.8°, 43.8°, 47.9° and 51.9°, corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) crystal facets of hexagonal phase CdS (JCPDS no. 77–2306) [37], respectively. The XRD patterns of other synthesized samples were in accordance with d-CdS fundamentally. Notably, no characteristic peaks of NCQDs were collected in CdS-xC patterns due to the low content and the high dispersity of NCQDs. In addition, it can be seen from the XRD patterns that no obvious diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 were observed in CdS-xC patterns, indicating that there was no impurity in composite photocatalysts.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the morphology of d-CdS presented smooth spheroid-like nanoparticles with a size of about 80 nm. For CdS-50C, as can be seen from the SEM image in Fig. 5b, the morphology and size were similar to those of d-CdS. The distinct difference could be observed from TEM images. The TEM images in Fig. 5c and d revealed that there were concave holes on the surface of CdS-50C but not on the surface of d-CdS. The HRTEM images were recorded for CdS-50C, Fig. 5e exhibited clear lattice fringes spacing of 0.333 and 0.359 nm, corresponding to the (002) and (100) lattice plane of hexagonal phase CdS, respectively, which were consistent with XRD patterns. In addition, CdS lattice fringes, NCQDs and concave holes could be simultaneously observed in Fig. 5f, indicating successful combination between NCQDs and CdS. EDS results in Fig. 5g showed that four elements, including Cd, S, C and O existed in CdS-50C. There was no N element in TEM-EDS, which might because NCQDs were dispersed in photocatalyst composite or the binding process of CdS and NCQDs led to decrement of N-containing functional groups.

N2 adsorption-desorption experiment was carried out to confirm the specific surface area and pore size distribution of d-CdS and CdS-50C. As shown in the Fig. 6 a, the N2 adsorption and desorption curves all presented the IV type curve and H3 type hysteresis loop. The specific surface areas of CdS-50C and d-CdS were determined to be 24.46 and 16.42 m2/g, respectively. The specific area of CdS-50C increased due to the concave hole on the surface of CdS-50C, which accorded with expectation. The Fig. 6b showed that the average pore diameter of CdS-50C was 14 nm, which was mesoporous. These results were consistent with previous analysis showed in TEM images. In contrast, the average pore diameter of d-CdS was 30 nm. Since no pores were observed on the surface of d-CdS from TEM images, the pore size might come from the slit holes formed by the stacking of sample particles. It was speculated that the spherical CdS was etched by ammonia generated from decomposition of g-C3N4 during hydrothermal process, which led to the formation of concave holes on CdS-50C. The increase of the specific surface area of the composite catalyst increased the contact area between solid and liquid during reaction, which was beneficial to the photocatalytic reaction.

FTIR and the Raman spectrum were conducted to investigate the chemical structures and surface information of synthesized samples. As showed in Fig. 7 a, the characteristic peaks around 1106, 1384 and 1637 cm−1 were corresponding to characteristic peaks of CdS. It was noting that obvious characteristic peaks were observed at 2850 and 2922 cm−1 for CdS-xC samples compared to d-CdS which were ascribed to the -CH2 stretching vibration of NCQDs, revealing the interaction between NCQDs and CdS. The absorption peak at 3437 cm−1 belonged to the breathing vibration of -OH of absorption water. The Raman spectra of d-CdS and CdS-50C were displayed in Fig. 7b. It can be seen that two peak at 305.4 and 605.6 cm−1 were presented which were corresponding to characteristic peak of CdS, and relevant to 1LO and 2LO model of CdS. Compared with d-CdS, all the peak intensities of CdS-50C decreased and the peak at 605.6 cm−1 shifted to higher wavenumber (608.3 cm−1). It might be the interaction between CdS and NCQDs that led to the difference of Raman peaks [38]. These results also accounted for the change of surface structural of composite.

XPS was performed to analyze the chemical composition and state of the photocatalysts. The XPS spectra of the as-synthesized d-CdS and CdS-50C were provided in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8a showed the existence of S, C and Cd element in both d-CdS and CdS-50C. No N element was obviously detected in CdS-50C sample because of the small amount and high dispersion of NCQDs on CdS surface. The Cd 3d high-resolution spectra in Fig. 8b showed that two peaks centered at 405.2 eV and 411.8 eV for d-CdS, which were assigned to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively. Notably, the peaks position of Cd 3d for CdS-50C exhibited positive shift compared with d-CdS. The S 2p spectra in Fig. 8c can be deconvoluted into two peaks where the peaks at 161.6 eV and 162.7 eV were originated from S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2−, respectively. Similar to Cd 3d, the binding energy of S 2p for Cd-50C shifted toward higher binding energy compared with d-CdS. These results suggested the formation of intimate interaction between NCQDs and CdS and the electron transfer from CdS to NCQDs at the interface. What's more, in the C 1 s high resolution spectrum of CdS-50C (Fig. 8d), the three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 286.7 and 288.7 eV were corresponding to C-C, C-S and O=C-O bond [39,40], respectively. The existence of C-S bond further proved the interaction between NCQDs and CdS.

Photocatalytic H2 production performance for d-CdS and CdS-xC composites was investigated in 10% lactic aqueous under visible-light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation with a xenon lamp. As displayed in Fig. 9 a, d-CdS showed a poor photocatalytic H2 production activity of 316.7 μmol/g/h, which could be attributed to the poor carrier migration and quick recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes. After combined with NCQDs, the H2 evolution performance for composites enhanced obviously compared with d-CdS. Among them, the optimized CdS-50C possessed the highest H2 evolution performance of 2306.1 μmol/g/h, which is about 7.3 times higher than that of d-CdS. It was ascribed to the NCQDs acting as electron acceptor to accelerate the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Notably, the experiment results showed further increasing NCQDs in samples led to decrease in H2 evolution performance. This could be attibuted to the excessive NCQDs which covered the active sites of hydrongen production and blocked light absorption for composites and hence limited the photocatalytic H2 production efficiency. Moreover, in order to explore the stability of as-synthezied photocatalyst, cyclic photocatalysis experiment for CdS-50C was carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, the rate of H2 evolution of CdS-50C decreased slightly during three successive photocatalytic reactions within 12 h. And the decrease of photocatalytic activity may be caused by the easy photocorrosion of CdS [41,42]. While the pattern XRD of CdS-50C shown in Fig. 9c indicated that there is no significant difference in the phase before and after the cyclic photocatalytic reaction.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was performed to study the light absorption properties and band structure of synthesized photocatalysts. Fig. 10 a showed the UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra of the synthesized samples. The absorption edge of d-CdS was 547 nm, which resulted from the intrinsic band gap absorption of hexagonal CdS. It was noteworthy that the absorption edge of CdS-xC composite photocatalysts exhibited red shift when compared with d-CdS and different samples presented various red shift for the different additive amount of NCQDs, indicating the band gaps decreased for CdS-xC composite photocatalysts, which might be attributed to the interface interaction between NCQDs and CdS as mentioned above. In addition, the absorbance of visible light increased for CdS-xC among 550–800 nm, which was beneficial to the absorption of light during photocatalytic process. We further calculated the band gap of samples according to Kubelk-Munk equation [43] and the curves of (αhυ)0.5 vs. hυ of samples were showed in Fig. 10b. The band gaps of as-synthesized photocatalysts were estimated from curves and showed in Table 1 . The above DRS results indicated that the deposited NCQDs dramatically affected the light absorption performance of materials.

PL spectroscopy was carried out to analyze the separation and recombination situation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. It is generally believed that the generation of fluorescence is originated from the radiation generated by the combination of electrons and hole. And the stronger fluorescence intensity means the higher degree of electron-hole combination. The PL spectra of the samples were measured at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm. As shown in the Fig. 11 a, the emission peak appeared at about 540 nm and the emission peak intensity of CdS-50C was obviously the lowest compared with other photocatalysts. The lower emission peak intensity proved that the electron-hole pairs recombination was largely suppressed in composite photocatalysts, and the electron-hole pairs could be effectively separated, thus facilitating the photocatalytic reaction. This was due to the combination of NCQDs with CdS accelerating electron transfer and hindering the process of electron and hole recombination.

The time-resolved photoluminescence spectra was recorded to study the charge carrier dynamics for synthesized samples. The decay curves were fitting to obtain the decay time on the basis of double exponential model and the average radiative lifetime was calculated according to the formula as follow:
τ av
=
∑
i = 1
n
A i
τ
i
2
∑
i = 1
n
A i
τ i
Where A is corresponding amplitude and τ represents for PL emission lifetime [44,45]. Fig. 11b showed the PL decay curves of the samples and the corresponding calculated average radiative lifetime were summarized in Table 2 . It can be seen that the PL decay rate of CdS-xC was obviously faster than that of d-CdS and the average radiative lifetime of the complex decreased with the increasing content of NCQDs. The decrease in average radiative lifetime for CdS-xC was due to the electrons excited to the conduction band of CdS quickly migrating to the NCQDs on the CdS surface, which corroborated that the NCQDs could serve as an efficient cocatalyst to capture photo-generated electrons and realize directional migration of photo-generated electrons. In view of XPS characterization result, the C-S bond between CdS and NCQDs acted as effective transfer pathways for the photo-generated electrons.

The electrochemical behavior of the synthesized photocatalysts was tested in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution to study the charge carriers separation and transfer efficiency. The transient photocurrent responses for samples were carried out under visible light with light source turned on and off alternately. As illustrated in Fig. 12 , the photocurrent density increased sharply when light was switched on for synthesized samples and they all exhibited reproducible photocurrent density during 10 cycles. It was obvious that the photocurrent density of composite photocatalysts were higher than that of d-CdS and the photocurrent density of CdS-50C was the strongest among them, demonstrating the introduction of NCQDs could facilitate the efficient transport of photo-generated electrons which was beneficial to photocatalytic reaction process. Furthermore, EIS measurement was conducted to elucidate electron transfer resistance. In Fig. 12b, the EIS Nyquist plots of CdS-xC presented smaller impedance radius than that of d-CdS. This difference suggested that the charge transfer resistances of composites were obviously decreased by introducing NCQDs onto CdS as cocatalyst and thus leading to high transfer efficiency of photo-generated carriers. The cycling voltammetry curves in Fig. 12c showed the anodic and cathodic current of CdS-50C increased sharply than that of d-CdS, proving the fast electron transfer effect of NCQDs.

In order to find out more information about the energy band structure of composite photocatalysts, the M-S curves and VB-XPS spectra were obtained. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 13 a that the slopes of the M-S curves of CdS-50C and d-CdS were positive, representing typical n-type semiconductor. The extrapolated results suggested the flat band were −0.23 V and −0.30 V for CdS-50C and d-CdS. Generally, the conduct band value for n-type semiconductor is approximately 0.1, 0.2 V more negative than flat band value [46–48], thus the conduct band value of CdS-50C and d-CdS could be calculated to be −0.33 V and −0.40 V, respectively. However, the VB-XPS spectra in Fig. 13b showed no significant difference for CdS-50C and d-CdS since the valent band value was 1.73 eV for both. These results indicated that the decrease of band gap for CdS-50C was due to decrease of conduction band position. Hence, the energy band structure of CdS-50C was illustrated in Fig. 14 .

On the basis of above analysis, the probable photocatalytic H2 evolution mechanism was proposed as follows: The photocatalyst produced the photo-generated electron-hole pairs upon the irradiation of visible light. The electron-hole pairs combined rapidly in pure CdS which resulted in it is low H2 production activity. After introducing the NCQDs on CdS surface, the light absorbance of CdS photocatalyst was enhanced due to the sensitization of NCQDs and light reflection in concave holes, contributing to produce more electrons under irradiation. Besides, the NCQDs can act as electron accepter for the lower potential and reduce the recombination of charge carrier, which suppressed the recombination of electron-hole pairs through C-S bond to facilitate electron transfer. Because of the appropriate conduct band potential, the separated electron reduced proton in water to generate H2 while the residual holes in photocatalyst were consumed by lactic acid. Thus the NCQDs/CdS composite photocatalysts exhibeted excellent H2 production performance than pure CdS.

In this paper, g-C3N4-derived NCQDs was applied as a photosensitizer and cocatalyst in photocatalysis. The NCQDs-modified CdS nanocomposite catalyst was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. By electron microscopy characterization, it was found that NCQDs with a size of about 3 nm were uniformly grown on CdS nanoparticles with a size of about 80 nm and there were obvious concave holes on the surface of composite catalyst. The results of FTIR, Raman and XPS showed strong interaction between NCQDs and CdS by means of the C-S bond. UV–Vis DRS confirmed that the introduction of NCQDs in composite catalyst could improve the light absorption capacity of the composite catalyst, enhancing the utilization efficiency of light. Moreover, the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes in the composite catalyst was suppressed, which could be effectively separated, and photo-generated electrons could transfer to the NCQDs to reduce H + to form H2. The photocatalytic water splitting experiment showed that the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of composite catalysts was improved under visible light, and the maximum hydrogen production rate reached 2306.7 μmol/g/h for CdS-50C, which was about 7 times the amount of unsupported NCQDs sample. This work provides a feasible route for synthesis of NCQDs from g-C3N4 and it is application for CdS photocatalyst.

Zhenning Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yanhong Wang: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jiayuan Zhuang: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yawen Li: Writing – review & editing. Lishan Jia: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the general program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21878251, 22078271). The authors thank Analysis and Testing Center of Xiamen University for the analysis and observation work in this study.
